Package org.apache.juneau.commons.bean

Annotation Interface BeanProp

@Documented @Target({FIELD,METHOD,PARAMETER}) @Retention(RUNTIME) @Inherited public @interface BeanProp
Annotation that can be applied to bean fields, getter/setter methods, and constructor parameters to control bean introspection and property modeling.

The bean-modeling sibling of @MarshalledProp. Where @MarshalledProp (in juneau-marshall) carries the marshalling-only attributes (such as dictionary, format, and properties), this annotation carries the attributes that describe the bean property itself — its name, accessibility (read/write only), concrete type, generic type parameters, element type, and the bean factory used to instantiate its value.

Splitting the annotations lets a property participate in the bean model (for example, to declare its name or read-only semantics) without dragging in any marshalling concerns, and lets the bean-modeling layer live in juneau-commons independent of juneau-marshall.

Can be used in the following locations:

  • Bean fields, getter methods, and setter methods.
  • Constructor parameters.
See Also:
  • @BeanProp Annotation

  • Optional Element Summary

    Optional Elements
    Modifier and Type
    Optional Element
    Description
    String[]
    description
    Optional description for the exposed API.
    Class<?>
    elementType
    Element type for streaming/consuming bean properties.
    Class<? extends BeanFactory>
    factory
    Bean factory class for this property's value.
    String
    name
    Identifies the name of the property.
    Class<?>[]
    params
    For bean properties of maps and collections, this annotation can be used to identify the class types of the contents of the bean property object when the generic parameter types are interfaces or abstract classes.
    String
    ro
    Identifies a property as read-only.
    String
    summary
    Short, concise summary of the exposed API.
    Class<?>
    type
    Identifies a specialized class type for the property.
    String
    value
    A synonym for name().
    String
    wo
    Identifies a property as write-only.

  • Element Details

    • description

      String[] description
      Optional description for the exposed API.
      Returns:
      The annotation value.
      Since:
      9.2.0
      Default:
      {}

    • summary

      String summary
      Short, concise summary of the exposed API.

      Intended as a brief, single-line description suitable for AI/LLM consumption, compact documentation, or any context where brevity matters. See Schema.summary() for the canonical definition; this field is the bean-property-level counterpart.

      Returns:
      The annotation value.
      Since:
      10.0.0
      Default:
      ""

    • elementType

      Class<?> elementType
      Element type for streaming/consuming bean properties.

      Specifies the element type for properties of type Stream, BeanSupplier, BeanConsumer, or BeanChannel when type erasure prevents the framework from inferring the generic type argument at runtime.

      This attribute also supports:

      • Narrowing - Specify a more specific subtype than the declared type
      • Concrete implementation - Specify a concrete class for an abstract/interface element type
      Example:

      public class OrderCollection { // Stream property - element type cannot be inferred at runtime due to erasure @BeanProp(elementType=Order.class) public Stream<Order> getOrders() { ... } // BeanChannel with concrete impl specified instead of abstract element type @BeanProp(elementType=ConcreteItem.class) public BeanChannel<AbstractItem> getItems() { ... } }

      See Also:
      • BeanSupplier
      • BeanConsumer
      • BeanChannel
      Returns:
      The annotation value.
      Default:
      void.class

    • factory

      Class<? extends BeanFactory> factory
      Bean factory class for this property's value.

      Specifies a BeanFactory class to use when instantiating the value of this specific bean property, overriding the class-level BeanType.factory() if present.

      When a factory class is specified, the framework resolves it in the following order:

      1. Look up the factory class in the configured BeanStore
      2. Attempt direct instantiation via no-arg constructor or getInstance() static method
      3. Throw IllegalArgumentException if both fail
      Example:

      public class MyBean { @BeanProp(factory=ItemChannelFactory.class, elementType=Item.class) public BeanChannel<Item> getItems() { ... } }

      See Also:
      • BeanFactory
      • BeanType.factory()
      Returns:
      The annotation value.
      Default:
      org.apache.juneau.commons.function.BeanFactory.Void.class

    • name

      String name
      Identifies the name of the property.

      Normally, this is automatically inferred from the field name or getter method name of the property. However, this property can be used to assign a different property name from the automatically inferred value.

      Dynamic beans

      On a field whose type is not a Map, "*" does not create a dynamic property: the property name is the field name (same as omitting name/value), while other attributes on this annotation still apply.

      The bean property named "*" is the designated "dynamic property" which allows for "extra" bean properties not otherwise defined.

      The following examples show how to define dynamic bean properties.

      // Option #1 - A simple public Map field. // The field name can be anything. public class BeanWithDynaField { @BeanProp(name="*") public Map<String,Object> extraStuff = new LinkedHashMap<>(); } // Option #2 - Getters and setters. // Method names can be anything. // Getter must return a Map with String keys. // Setter must take in two arguments. public class BeanWithDynaMethods { @BeanProp(name="*") public Map<String,Object> getMyExtraStuff() { ... } @BeanProp(name="*") public void setAnExtraField(String name, Object value) { ... } }

      Note that the @Name annotation can also be used for identifying a property name.
      Returns:
      The annotation value.
      Default:
      ""

    • params

      Class<?>[] params
      For bean properties of maps and collections, this annotation can be used to identify the class types of the contents of the bean property object when the generic parameter types are interfaces or abstract classes.
      Example:

      public class MyBean { // Identify concrete map type with String keys and Integer values. @BeanProp(type=HashMap.class, params={String.class,Integer.class}) public Map p1; }

      Returns:
      The annotation value.
      Default:
      {}

    • ro

      String ro
      Identifies a property as read-only.

      Serializers will serialize such properties as usual, but parsers will silently ignore them.

      Example:

      public class MyBean { @BeanProp(ro="true") public float price; }

      Notes:
      • Java Records: Marking record components as read-only is not supported during parsing. Because records are immutable, all components must be provided to the canonical constructor. Read-only components will be serialized as usual, but the parser will be unable to instantiate the record if the read-only component values are missing from the input.
      Returns:
      The annotation value.
      Default:
      ""

    • type

      Class<?> type
      Identifies a specialized class type for the property.

      Normally this can be inferred through reflection of the field type or getter return type. However, you'll want to specify this value if you're parsing beans where the bean property class is an interface or abstract class to identify the bean type to instantiate. Otherwise, you may cause an InstantiationException when trying to set these fields.

      This property must denote a concrete bean class with a no-arg constructor.

      Example:

      public class MyBean { // Identify concrete map type. @BeanProp(type=HashMap.class) public Map p1; }

      Returns:
      The annotation value.
      Default:
      void.class

    • value

      String value
      A synonym for name().

      The following annotations are equivalent:

      @BeanProp(name="foo") @BeanProp("foo")

      Returns:
      The annotation value.
      Default:
      ""

    • wo

      String wo
      Identifies a property as write-only.

      Parsers will parse such properties as usual, but serializers will silently ignore them.

      Example:

      public class MyBean { @BeanProp(wo="true") public float price; }

      Returns:
      The annotation value.
      Default:
      ""
